If they'd be different, then I think people could write both a personal vision statement and a statement for each of their projects — personal statement on their personal site, project statement on each project site. For me, I'd just write one statement, since the vision guiding my project is more interesting than my personal vision (they're basically the same, but the project vision is more specific).

I imagine that the personal statement is the one you should link in your slack profile. A project statement (if you felt like writing one) is something you could link to, for example, as part of a relevant discussion to help folks understand a point you're making.

I imagine that the personal statement is the one you should link in your slack profile. A project statement (if you felt like writing one) is something you could link to, for example, as part of a relevant discussion to help folks understand a point you're making.
We're trying to hit that exact group with ValOS, but we currently severely lack system documentation, streamlined publishing, project management related tools and optimization. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

I would say a major problem in general is incidental complexity. When you want to actually make something working, you are quickly faced all sorts of issues with coding IDEs, database setups, server environment configurations, etc. They don't really have much at all to do with "how my program does things", but rather they are "how do I set up an environment where my program can actually work in", which isn't all that interesting to someone who wants to create Their Thing. I believe that to be a major obstacle for the group you mention.
<@UG0EL18H3> I'd say Max/MSP and Pure Data directly target that "dangerous enough" kind of computer power user.
<@UGK6P07UP> Do you have a link to ValOS?

right, i had almost forgotten about Max and PD! i guess there are tools like LiveCode as well, but i hesitate to think of that as a humane medium of software creation. hypercard is probably the closest thing that exists to what i have in my mind, but the big trouble with it was lack of integration into the underlying OS. what valaa is doing is very interesting to me, and i can appreciate why it is cloud-based, it’s just so tempting to want to use the capabilities of the OS running on my machine rather than someone else’s

attachments.title: Chapter 1: The Way Houyhnhnms Compute
attachments.text: Dear fellow programmer, I used to think humans wrote software the way they did because they knew what they were doing. Then I realized that they didn't really know, but adopted ways that seemed to work better than others. Or maybe rather humans were adop...

text: I had a formal path, I'm afraid. I leaned programming because I got into a CS major. Only after getting in did I realize I liked it.

Constant troubles with:
- writing async code and any code where the control flow is not obvious so it is MUCH harder to reason about
- write tests instead generating them / using formal methods & proofs
- expressing the business models and ideas
- better ecosystem
<@UG0EL18H3> What about you?
Same as Kartik: I fell in love with it the moment I started learning it.

i (naively) wanted to write my own operating system. i couldn't afford a Mac at the time (very early 2000s) and didn't have access to the internet, but i really wanted to use Mac OS because it was so much more beautiful and intuitive than windows. so i decided to write my own. i was in my early teens then and the only tool i knew how to use was Powerpoint, so i cobbled together a little clone of the Mac desktop in powerpoint and linked the slides together with buttons and hyperlinks (sort of how prototyping in Sketch works, now that I think about it...). after building this massive PPT file and having it corrupt itself, i realized i needed to learn how software was _actually_ built, hence, i made it my goal to become a programmer :slightly_smiling_face:
wanted to know how the snake game from early nokia phones was done. Learned C and implemented it over and over
YES, SNAKE GAME omg so many memories

I will say that I learned a lot of graphical programming and how to make games, all via my TI-83+ graphing calculator. Also used it to teach myself how to use 2D drawing commands to render 3D graphics

But to start out, I made a tic-tac-toe game with layers and layers of nested "if" statements to check *every possible combination* of whether someone had "won"
oh man, i can practically feel that giant ball of nested If:Then:End. ti-basic got me through math class too haha

I was looking at procedurally generated 3D printed sculptures created by Nervous System, and I wanted to learn how to make my own sculptures that looked like that. I initially learned OpenSCAD, but it's not powerful enough to do what I want, so I created my own language.

To me, a “pitch” suggests the purpose is to convince this (kinda narrow) audience of the project’s value. I’d prefer an answer to “What’s your perspective and approach?” rather than “Why should I want to use this?” The later is also a pretty hard question for a novel project to convincingly answer without a demo.

I like the argument, but don't understand the list. Why list the desktop computer and not smartphones or tablets? I suspect that the desktop computer probably won't exist in its current form for decades to come, and something like the smartphone will ... but that doesn't follow from his argument.

What do people think of designing languages that would have been great to have 30 years ago? As a design pattern/thought exercise. The more I think about it the more I like it. Last night I experimented with taking a document from the 1600's and saying "what would have been a useful grammar for this type of thing" back then. To try and inform good languages for 2050.

What do people think of designing languages that would have been great to have 30 years ago? As a design pattern/thought exercise. The more I think about it the more I like it. Last night I experimented with taking a document from the 1600's and saying "what would have been a useful grammar for this type of thing" back then. To try and inform good languages for 2050.
(Of course, that's 400 years ago, but the idea of designing for the past in order to design for the future is an interesting way to look at it, I think )
(Of course, that's 400 years ago, but the idea of designing for the past in order to design for the future is an interesting way to look at it, I think )

The thought I am noodling around this is that you have some way of expressing programs at various levels of abstraction. So as I gain experience and knowledge I start to dig into the lower levels. Maybe this starts with something like boxes and arrows or cells in a spreadsheet but then I can dig deeper, eventually getting to the details of how a sort algorithm works, for example.

text: To answer your question, I was 10 when I said I wanted to make video games and my dad sat me down and explained I couldn't do that because I'd have to hire a team of illustrators and programmers, and then when I was 11 found out I could program a Commodore 64 to output anything I wanted with a series of "print" statements. Exciting stuff! I went on to make a series of amateur Arkanoid clones, until I realized the programming languages I was using (C++ and VB) kinda suck and need improvement, which led me on a path that led to <http://loyc.net|loyc.net>.
